pattern: Re-expressing every value by where it lands in the second array turns the two-array ordering condition into a single-array question of counting increasing triplets, which a pair of Fenwick trees can answer in one left-to-right and one right-to-left sweep.
hints: Record, for every value, its position in the second array; then walk the first array and replace each value with that recorded position, giving one combined sequence to reason about.
A triplet is good exactly when three positions in that combined sequence, taken in the order the first array visits them, come out strictly increasing.

For every position, count how many earlier entries in the combined sequence are smaller and how many later entries are larger using a Fenwick tree in each direction, then multiply and sum those two counts across all positions.

edge_inputs.description: minimum size, both already the identity permutation
minimum size, second array fully reversed so nothing can be good
both arrays identical and fully increasing
first array fully reversed relative to the second
both arrays fully reversed, so every position still disagrees
a single adjacent swap in the second array breaks some triplets but not all
minimum size with exactly one good triplet possible

stress.description: random permutation pairs up to the maximum length
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 100000))
    nums1 = list(range(n))
    rng.shuffle(nums1)
    nums2 = list(range(n))
    rng.shuffle(nums2)
    return [nums1, nums2]

stress.description: nums2 is a small number of swaps away from matching nums1 exactly
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 100000))
    nums1 = list(range(n))
    rng.shuffle(nums1)
    nums2 = list(nums1)
    swaps = max(1, n // 20)
    for _ in range(swaps):
        i = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        j = rng.randint(0, n - 1)
        nums2[i], nums2[j] = nums2[j], nums2[i]
    return [nums1, nums2]
